title: Embarrassingly Simple Self-Distillation Improves Code Generation
gist: Apple researchers show that a large language model can substantially improve its own code-generation ability using only its own sampled outputs -- no verifier, teacher model, or reinforcement learning required -- via 'simple self-distillation' (SSD), lifting Qwen3-30B-Instruct's pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6 from 42.4% to 55.3% and generalizing across model families and sizes.

themes.theme: Self-improvement without RL or a teacher model

insights.claim: Simple self-distillation (SSD) improves an LLM's code generation by sampling solutions from the model itself under specific temperature/truncation settings, then fine-tuning the same model on those self-generated samples with standard supervised fine-tuning -- with no verifier, teacher model, or reinforcement learning involved.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A materially cheaper post-training path than RLHF/RLVR pipelines (no reward model, no RL infrastructure, no external verifier) -- lowers the cost and complexity bar for improving code-gen models, which matters for smaller labs or on-device model iteration.
insights.quote: Can a large language model (LLM) improve at code generation using only its own raw outputs, without a verifier, a teacher model, or reinforcement learning? We answer in the affirmative with simple self-distillation (SSD)

insights.claim: SSD improves Qwen3-30B-Instruct's pass@1 accuracy on LiveCodeBench v6 from 42.4% to 55.3%, with gains concentrated on harder problems.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: A roughly 13-point absolute pass@1 jump from a self-only training loop is a large result for a technique this cheap; a strong signal that self-distillation deserves a place in any code-model post-training stack.
insights.quote: SSD improves Qwen3-30B-Instruct from 42.4% to 55.3% pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6, with gains concentrating on harder problems

insights.claim: SSD generalizes across both the Qwen and Llama model families at 4B, 8B, and 30B parameter scales, and works on both instruct and 'thinking' (reasoning) model variants.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Broad generalization across model families/sizes/variants suggests SSD is a robust, widely-applicable technique rather than a narrow trick tuned to one model -- increases confidence it could be adopted industry-wide as a standard post-training step.
insights.quote: it generalizes across Qwen and Llama models at 4B, 8B, and 30B scale, including both instruct and thinking variants

insights.claim: The authors trace SSD's gains to a 'precision-exploration conflict' in LLM decoding, showing SSD reshapes token distributions in a context-dependent way -- suppressing distractor probability-tails where precision matters while preserving useful diversity where exploration matters.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Provides a mechanistic explanation (not just an empirical result) for why self-distillation works, which strengthens confidence that the technique is generalizable and not a benchmark-specific artifact, and offers a conceptual lens ('precision-exploration conflict') applicable to other decoding-quality problems.
insights.quote: we trace these gains to a precision-exploration conflict in LLM decoding and show that SSD reshapes token distributions in a context-dependent way, suppressing distractor tails where precision matters while preserving useful diversity where exploration matters
